Pod Issues (STG)

When Hownd or Yodlee Fails, provide a fallback provider.

Purpose

This feature provides the ability for Credit Sense to call Hownd for a 2nd time, with a fallback provider, either the website (e.g. CBA) or Yodlee (attached as a sub context). This is does to maintain a high success rate, where one or the other service providers may be able to extract data.

Overview

There are three parts to this solution:

  1. Accepting a new value on the /restartJob

  2. Storing that information in the jobAttempt data,

  3. Using that information to determine which ObjectiveDefs to include

New post value name added to both jobStart and jobRestart end-points (see API Spec below):

  • preferredContextCode

API Spec

Table Changes

Objective Resolve

The priority is used to determine which ObjectiveDefs to select (lower number = higher priority).

preferredContextCode represents a specific context that should be used. It accepts NULL value to select a default service provider. If it is not null, jobs should be executed against the preferred service.

Plan: Complex Form Post Support for API Requests

Overview

Extend the existing API execution infrastructure in planExecuteMany.ts to support complex form-encoded HTTP requests with context-specific templates. This enables authentication flows like Yodlee that require nested form data structures with session tokens and dynamic field arrays.

Example Login Form

cobSessionToken: {cobSessionToken}
userSessionToken: {userSessionToken}
siteId:3857
credentialFields[0].enclosedType:com.yodlee.common.FieldInfoSingle
credentialFields[0].displayName:Client Number
credentialFields[0].fieldType.typeName:IF_LOGIN
credentialFields[0].helpText:97294
credentialFields[0].maxlength:8
credentialFields[0].name:LOGIN
credentialFields[0].valueIdentifier:LOGIN
credentialFields[0].valueMask:LOGIN_FIELD
credentialFields[0].isEditable:true
credentialFields[0].size:20
credentialFields[0].value:{{ LOGIN VALUE }}
credentialFields[1].enclosedType:com.yodlee.common.FieldInfoSingle
credentialFields[1].displayName:Password
credentialFields[1].fieldType.typeName:IF_PASSWORD
credentialFields[1].helpText:97293
credentialFields[1].maxlength:16
credentialFields[1].name:PASSWORD
credentialFields[1].valueIdentifier:PASSWORD
credentialFields[1].valueMask:LOGIN_FIELD
credentialFields[1].isEditable:true
credentialFields[1].size:20
credentialFields[1].value:{{PASSWORD VALUE}}

Challenges

  • Each login form is specific to a Bank (Context), therefore should be considered unique per Context

  • How to store and where to get SiteId from

  • How to store the login structure

Approach

Phase 1: Database Schema Extension

Extend existing API specification tables to store form templates per context, enabling flexible configuration without code changes.

Phase 2: Form Template Processing

Enhance the processAPIPlanInstructions method around line 700 to detect and process form-encoded requests using database-stored templates.

Phase 3: Session Data Integration

Leverage existing shared data mechanisms from objectiveResolve.ts to extract session tokens (cobSession, userSession) from job records.

Phase 4: Backward Compatibility

Maintain full compatibility with existing JSON API requests while adding form post capabilities through content-type detection.

Required Database Changes

The stored procedures and operations will need to be clearly commented to say they are specific to Yodlee.

Table Modifications

Option B: New Dedicated Table (Alternative)

-- Create dedicated context form template table
CREATE TABLE mContextYodleeFormTemplate (
    contextYodleeFormTemplateId UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    contextId VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    templateName VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'login',
    formTemplate JSONB NOT NULL,
    contentType VARCHAR(100) DEFAULT 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded',
    templateVersion INTEGER DEFAULT 1,
    createDt TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
    updateDt TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
    UNIQUE(contextCode, templateName, templateVersion)
);

-- Indexes for fast lookups
CREATE INDEX idx_mContextYodleeFormTemplate_lookup 
ON mContextYodleeFormTemplate(contextId, templateName);

CREATE INDEX idx_mContextYodleeFormtemplateVersion 
ON mContextYodleeFormTemplate(contextId, templateVersion DESC);

Sample Data Structure

{
  "contextCode": "CBA",
  "templateName": "login",
  "formTemplate": {
    "cobSessionToken": "{{ cobSession }}",
    "userSessionToken": "{{ userSession }}",
    "siteId": "{{siteId}}",
    "credentialFields": [
      {
        "enclosedType": "com.yodlee.common.FieldInfoSingle",
        "displayName": "Client Number",
        "fieldType.typeName": "IF_LOGIN",
        "helpText": "97294",
        "maxlength": 8,
        "name": "LOGIN",
        "valueIdentifier": "LOGIN",
        "valueMask": "LOGIN_FIELD",
        "isEditable": true,
        "size": 20,
        "value": "{{ LOGIN VALUE }}"
      },
      {
        "enclosedType": "com.yodlee.common.FieldInfoSingle",
        "displayName": "Password",
        "fieldType.typeName": "IF_PASSWORD",
        "helpText": "97293",
        "maxlength": 16,
        "name": "PASSWORD",
        "valueIdentifier": "PASSWORD",
        "valueMask": "LOGIN_FIELD",
        "isEditable": true,
        "size": 20,
        "value": "{{PASSWORD VALUE}}"
      }
    ]
  },
  "contentType": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded",
  "requiresSessionData": true
}

Required Database Operations

New Operations Needed

// For Option B (Dedicated Table)  
- contextFormTemplateGetOperation
- contextFormTemplateCreateOperation
- contextFormTemplateUpdateOperation
- contextFormTemplateGetManyByContextOperation

Sample Operation Interface

export class ContextFormTemplateGetInput {
    @Prop.string()
    public contextCode!: string
    
    @Prop.string(() => [{ optional: true }])
    public templateName?: string
    
    @Prop.boolean(() => [{ optional: true }])
    public isActive?: boolean
}

export class ContextFormTemplateGetOutput {
    @Prop.uuid()
    public contextFormTemplateId!: string
    
    @Prop.string()
    public contextCode!: string
    
    @Prop.string()
    public templateName!: string
    
    @Nested(() => [{ type: 'object' }])
    public formTemplate!: Record<string, any>
    
    @Prop.string()
    public contentType!: string
    
    @Prop.boolean()
    public requiresSessionData!: boolean
}

Implementation Details

Enhanced API Plan Processing

Modify processAPIPlanInstructions method:

/**
 * Enhanced form post detection and processing
 * @TECH-DEBT: Form template processing for complex YODLEE API authentication
 * @TODO: TBD
 * @IMPACT: High - enables complex form posts across all API contexts
 * @EFFORT: Medium - requires database integration and template processing
 */
private async processAPIPlanInstructions(
    instructions: Instruction_PlanCreateManyInput[],
    plan: Plan_PlanCreateManyInput,
    apiDomainId: string,
    address: string,
    jobAttemptId: string,
    planId: string,
    jobId: string
): Promise<{
    executeDt: string
    planId: string
    planResultCode: sPlanResultCode
    planStatusCode: sPlanStatusCode
} | null>

Key Methods to Implement

1. Form Template Retrieval

private async getFormTemplate(
    contextCode: string, 
    templateName: string = 'login'
): Promise<FormTemplateConfig | null>

2. Form Data Building

private async buildFormDataFromDatabase(
    recordValues: Record<string, any>,
    contextCode: string,
    templateName: string = 'login'
): Promise<string>

Integration Points

Session Data Integration

  • Leverage existing shared data mechanism from getSharedRecordValues method around line 150

  • Extract session tokens from job attempt records using established patterns

  • Support both context-specific and job-level session data

Parameter Processing

  • Extend existing SEND_API_PARAM instruction processing

  • Support nested parameter mapping (e.g., credentialFields[0].value)

  • Maintain compatibility with existing URL and header parameters

Error Handling

  • Integrate with existing HTTP fault codes in planExecuteMany.ts

  • Use established error patterns for CONSUMER_COMMUNICATION_ISSUE and DEV_ERROR

  • Provide clear error messages for template and session data issues

Technical Debt

Current Tech Debt to Address

// @TECH-DEBT: This is a temporary hardcoded fix to get Yodlee shared data

// @IMPACT: Medium - will be resolved by this form template system

New Tech Debt Introduced

// @IMPACT: Low - performance optimization for high-volume contexts

Development

Phase 1

Part 1: Database Schema

  • Deploy database schema changes

  • Create initial form templates for Yodlee context

  • Implement database operations

  • Implement form detection and processing logic

  • Add session data extraction

  • Integrate with existing API execution flow

Part 2: Testing & Refinement

  • Comprehensive testing with Yodlee integration

  • Performance optimization

  • Error handling refinement

Phase 2

Long term solution for Login form management

Template Management UI

  • Admin interface for template syncing and modifying

    • Sync with Yodlee

    • Sync with CS (for CS Web and CS V3)

  • Template versioning and rollback capabilities (possibly)

Risks & Mitigations

Risk: Breaking Existing API Requests

Mitigation: Maintain strict backward compatibility through content-type detection and feature flags

Risk: Session Data Security

Mitigation: Leverage existing secure record value handling and encryption patterns

Risk: Template Configuration Complexity

Mitigation: Provide clear documentation on how the template will be used

Using the SQL Server Profiler

Reality Check

Outcome

  •  

Can the stored procedure be made more efficient without changing logic?

  •  

Can the stored procedure be restructured to make it more efficient?

  •  

Are there missing indexes

If the initial investigation of the above items improves the performance sufficiently then optimisation efforts can stop here.

Database and Application

Using Job Result Logging

If the stored procedure is remains a performance issue, then the database and application developers will work together. At this point the database developer will include additional logging on the specific stored procedure to record inputs and outputs.

Once this additional database logging is added, the application and database developer agree on which job/job attempt to re-run in order to collect useful and detailed information.

Reality Check

Outcome

  •  

Is the stored procedure being called more times than expected?

  •  

Do the number of rows match what is expected throughout the job attempt?

  •  

Does the data within each row match what is expected throughout the job attempt?

  •  

Can the data within each row be reduce?

If the initial investigation of the above items improves the performance sufficiently then optimisation efforts can stop here.

Database Caching

If there are no further opportunities to improve performance consider caching as an option.

Outcome

Functional Rollout

Regression & Dark Testing

What is Hownd?

Hownd is a web scraper designed to extract financial transaction data from a cutomers’s internet banking.

Why does Hownd exist? (aka The Business Problem)

Following the Global Financial Crisis (between mid 2007 and early 2009), the government introduced the passed the National Consumer Credit Protection Act 2009, which requires lenders to make reasonable enquiry about the suitability of a loan for a borrower, including an assessment of their ability to repay it.

This is typically achieved by obtaining and reviewing 90 days worth of a customer’s transaction history before providing a loan.

Retrieving this type of data can be challenging: banks typically do not provide an API, so the only viable solution is Web Scraping, which involves using a programmable web browser to read data from the banking site.

In order to help our clients fulfil this obligation, Credit Sense has been leveraging the services of a third party company, Yodlee.

While Yodlee’s services are successful in adding value to Credit Sense’s offering, they also present several challenges:

  1. Yodlee is an added cost to the business

  2. Yodlee’s services are an interruction to an otherwise linear supply chain process

  3. Yodlee is geared more towards non-time critical data aggregation:

    • Yodlee is slow

      • to retrieve the data from the source

      • to add new sources of data

      • to adapt to changes in old data sources

    • Yodlee’s data accuracy is sometimes unreliable

    • Yodlee fails to detect processing failures

    • Yodlee’s support is not responsive, which impacts our SLA

    • Yodlee adds a considerable amount of time overhead to day-to-day operation by managing their technical inefficiencies (1.5 FTE required to validate & manage Yodlee data)

  4. Yodlee cannot retrieve PDF statements from online banking accounts

CreditSense needs to be able retrieve data from any source on behalf of our client's customers as fast and as accurately as is possible.

A custom-built solution is the most effective approach: enters Hownd.

Installing Remote Explorer and Dev Containers Extensions in VS Code

Prerequisites

  • Ensure you have Visual Studio Code installed. You can download it from: https://code.visualstudio.com/

1. Installing the Remote Explorer Extension

The Remote Explorer is part of the "Remote Development" extension pack by Microsoft, which enables you to work with remote environments.

Steps:

  1. Open Visual Studio Code.

  2. Go to the Extensions view by clicking the Extensions icon in the Activity Bar on the side of the window or pressing Ctrl+Shift+X.

  3. In the search bar, type Remote - SSH or Remote Development.

  4. Find the extension named Remote Development pack by Microsoft and click Install.

  5. Find the extension Remote Explorer pack by Microsoft and click Install

 The Remote Development extension pack includes four extensions

See the following articles to get started with each of them:

  • Remote - SSH - Connect to any location by opening folders on a remote machine/VM using SSH.

  • Dev Containers - Work with a separate toolchain or container-based application inside (or mounted into) a container.

  • WSL - Get a Linux-powered development experience in the Windows Subsystem for Linux.

  • Remote - Tunnels - Connect to a remote machine via a secure tunnel, without configuring SSH.

HINT:

Ensure the remote explorer is visible in the sidebar by ticking the box in the context menu that appears when you right click in the sidebar.
Below is a very basic overview of the separate projects and services that define Hownd v3.

This release contains major breaking changes, and will more than likely disrupt traffic.

1. Phases

Phase 1

Single bank without MFA (as Esmund will not be around for an amount of the sprint and will be unable to assist with configuring MFA).

Phase 2

Start introducing volume and banks that require MFA.

2. Overview

  • Group overlap banks into 3 cohorts by 24‑hr volume

  • For each bank:

    • Apply Stage 0–4 ramp (with defined volumes and time windows).

    • Track metrics per bank and stage.

    • Require explicit go/no‑go before each ramp change.

  • Cohort order:

    • Cohort 1 (high volume: WBC)

    • Cohort 2 (medium volume: ANZNZ, ASB, STG, BNZ, UBank, Bankwest)

    • Cohort 3 (low volume: FICU, GBS, MAQ, TCB, AOT, WPB, QTMB)

    • Cohort 4 (high volume: CBA, WBC, ANZ AU, BEN)

2.1 Inputs and Assumptions

From the Hownd services table (Australia + NZ), you currently have these overlap banks (HOWND default, YODLEE backup) with example 24 hr volumes:

  • Hownd Supported Services in Australia and New Zealand

2.2 Bank Grouping & Order

2.3.1 Overlap Banks – rollout order

Use this order so that:

  • Start with modest volume to validate real‑world behaviour quickly

  • Leaving the highest‑risk banks (CBA, WBC, ANZ AU) until telemetry and confidence are confirmed

Proposed order (by cohort):

  1. Cohort 1 – Medium‑volume, AU/NZ mix:
    Goal: validate typical flows, MFA, and PDFs without massive support impact.

  1. Cohort 2 – Medium‑volume, AU/NZ mix:
    Goal: validate typical flows, MFA, and PDFs without massive support impact.

  1. Cohort 2 – Long tail / lower volume:
    Goal: broaden coverage and catch edge cases in lower‑traffic banks.

  1. Cohort 4 – High‑volume majors:
    Goal: largest impact; only proceed once cohorts 1–2 are stable.

Within each cohort, you still treat each bank separately for activation and rollback, but you move through the cohort in a controlled order.

As a web scraper, Hownd faces several challenges due to the unpredictable and constantly changing nature of websites.

Unlike APIs, which have standard structures and are typically thoroughly documented, websites are tailored for human interaction and often lack stable, machine-readable formats.

As websites evolve, scrapers built with fixed instructions may fail if the page layout or content changes, making it challenging to consistently capture accurate data.

To manage these challenges, developers often use reactive scrapers that detect specific states (like a login prompt) and respond accordingly. However, this approach can still struggle when websites make subtle, ongoing changes. For example, a banking site might change from infinite scrolling to pagination, which could cause a scraper to miss some transactions without clear errors.

Hownd’s approach is:

  • Insensitive to noise

  • Highly sensitive to critical change

This strategy relies on skilled human oversight to differentiate between minor and significant changes, ensuring data accuracy over strict automation.

Hownd’s mission

Hownd focuses on five key areas critical to effective data collection from websites:

  • Responsiveness: Since websites are highly dynamic and can change without notice, the system must adapt to new layouts, requirements, and any issues in real time. It

  • Scalability: The system should support a large number of data sources and concurrent scraping jobs, adapting to diverse pathways, unfamiliar pages, and data structures. Configuration must be intuitive for non-technical operators, enabling a small agile team to manage high volumes efficiently.

  • Accuracy: Ensuring precise, reliable data is crucial. The system must identify when data cannot be found, recognise conflicts, and prevent subtle website changes from altering the data's meaning. Ongoing validation for page changes ensure that data is always accurate.

  • Extensibility: The platform is designed for continuous improvement, allowing the addition of new features and services. This approach supports business growth and adaptability.

  • Security: Compliant with regulatory requirements, the system manages end-user consent and respects private data, adhering to legal standards and ISO compliance.

What is a Consumer?

A consumer is an entity that interacts with users to complete specific jobs or objectives.
It acts as an intermediary between the user and Hownd, gathering necessary information, communicating with the system, and returning results.

Credit Sense is the current consumer for Hownd, responsible for collecting and processing data.

This consumer model could be expanded or adapted in the future to handle additional jobs beyond Credit Sense.

What is the role of a Consumer?

The consumer is responsible for gathering job details from the customer and engaging with Hownd.

For example, in the V3 Customer Journey, the consumer prompts the customer for the necessary data to complete their requested job (for example, login details to access the customer’s online banking)

After interacting with the user, the consumer collects detailed information about the job at hand and sends it to Hownd in the form of a Payload.

What does a Payload look like?

The Payload received by Hownd typically includes these details:

  • Context: typically the bank’s shortcode (i.e. ‘CBA’)

  • Credentials: The customer’s login details to access their Online Banking (these are encrypted and stored in Vault)

  • Goal (and Objectives): A goal is broken down into Objectives. These objectives must be achieved for the goal to be considered successful.

Objectives are sub-goals.

A Goal could be ‘Retrieving Accounts & Transactions’
An Objective for this Goal could be ‘Retrieve bank Account Owner details’

  • Date Range: The consumer must consider a specific date range when collecting data. This range could span anywhere from a few weeks to up to 365 days, depending on the requirements of the job.

It is worth noting that Hownd transforms this payload and turns it into a Frame before reading it.

Everything that Hownd handles is or becomes a Frame.

How are results returned?

Once the job is completed, Hownd sends the results back to the consumer, which then communicates the outcome to the user or customer. These results indicate the success or failure of the job, depending on whether all objectives were met.

The outcome could be:

  • total_success: All objectives were completed successfully.

  • partial_success: Some objectives were completed, but not all.

  • failure/error/issue: Some or all objectives could not be completed.

    • A failure is typically something out of Hownd’s control, like incorrect credentials.

    • An error is typically an application or database problem that requires code fixes

    • An issue typically relates to configuration problems in the Operator Console.

The consumer receives a payload containing the results object from Hownd. This object outlines the completion status of the job, provides the extracted data and details on any errors or successes.

What is HD-Job?

HD-Job is a core component of Hownd and it is responsible for orchestrating and executing jobs.

It serves as the central control unit, ensuring that the necessary steps are taken to complete a job according to the user's goals and objectives.

Acting as the "brain" of the operation, HD-Job interacts with multiple services and systems to gather the information it needs and coordinate actions and execute jobs.

  • Key Services HD-Job Works With:

    • Consumer: Provides the job details (Context, Credentials, Goals) for execution.

    • HD-Asset: Provides HD-Job with instances of browsers that can perform the job.

    • HD-Blueprint: Provides the instructions on how to navigate and interact with the components found on the Bank’s website, so the goal’s objectives can be achieved

    • HD-Browser: Executes the navigation and interaction within the web environment.

The Frame Concept

A frame typically refers to a specific state or snapshot of a webpage or its contents at a given moment during the scraping process.

This "frame" captures a change or an update on the website, which might include a page load, dynamic content updates, or any interaction that results in the website displaying new or updated information.

Each frame is read to see what’s in it

Just like a movie they are processed in sequence as they arrive. In Hownd Frames may include login details, buttons to click on or just information to read.

HD-Job only understands frames, so every part of the job, from the consumer payload to the asset receipt to the browser pages, is translated into frames.

Each frame is handled correctly in sequence, guiding the entire process from start to finish.

How Does HD-Job Work?

HD Job follows a structured See / Think / Act cycle to execute jobs effectively.

This cycle ensures that each part of the job is completed in a methodical way, from gathering information to taking action.

When a frame arrives to goes through up to 3 steps 1) See, 2) Think and 3) Act.

▶️

▶️

▶️

Here's how it works:

  1. See:

    • HD-Job first observes the environment by gathering all necessary information.
      This includes retrieving necessary data from web pages, and transforming its structure from a DOM structure to a hierarchy of meaning that matches the Goal requirements.

    • During this phase, all data retrievals are processed together (i.e. in ‘parallel’), ensuring efficient collection of information.

  2. Think:

    • Once the necessary data is collected, HD-Job analyses and interprets it based on the objectives outlined in the job.

    • It processes each piece of data one at a time (i.e. ‘serial’), focusing on extracting and understanding the relevant details needed to complete the goal from the most recent Frame received.

  3. Act:

    • The action phase comes into play when HD-Job needs to trigger a new cycle or proceed with the next objective.

    • This occurs when a new frame needs to be initiated, such as navigating to a different page or performing an action on the website.

By processing frames in an iterative way via the See/Think/Act cycle , HD-Job can ensure that all objectives are ticked off methodically.

  • hd-job

What is HD-Browser?

HD-Browser is a part of Hownd and is responsible for rendering websites and carrying out actions on them.

It interacts directly with the website, performing tasks like clicking buttons, copying data, and sending back the results to HD-Job in the form of frames.

Essentially, HD-Browser is the "worker" that executes the instructions provided by HD-Job to complete a job.

How does HD-Browser Work?

  1. Rendering the Website:

    • HD-Browser loads the website and displays it in a way that allows HD-Job to interact with it.

    • This is the first step in completing any job, as the browser needs to be able to view and understand the webpage's structure.

  2. Performing Actions on the Website:

    • Once the page is loaded, HD-Browser can perform various actions on the website, such as:

      • Clicking buttons or links

      • Filling out forms

      • Interacting with dynamic content like pop-ups or modal windows

    These actions are necessary to retrieve the data or achieve the objectives outlined in the job.

  3. Sending Frames:

    • After completing each task, HD-Browser sends frames back to HD-Job, providing the necessary results to move the process forward.

Learn more about HD-Browser here: hd-browser

What is HD-Operator Console?

The HD-Operator Console (also known as ‘OpCon’) is the interface through which Hownd is configured and managed by operators.

As Hownd is a configuration-over-code platform, all setup and adjustments are done here through an intuitive, no-code interface.

This makes it possible for operators to teach Hownd how to interact with specific scenarios, navigate websites, and perform necessary actions that lead to job success.

How Does HD-Operator Console Work?

  • Teaching Hownd:

    • Operators use OpCon to train Hownd on how to interact with specific scenarios. This involves:

      • Navigation: Guiding Hownd through the steps needed to move around websites, click buttons, input data, or find elements.

      • Action Execution: Configuring what actions Hownd should take to achieve successful job completion.

  • Using the Repair Page:

    • The Repair Page in OpCon is where operators can observe jobs in real-time, pause their progress, replay them, and debug any issues. This ensures tasks are executed correctly and allows for quick adjustments if problems occur.

  • Frame-Based Management:

    • Just like all services in the Hownd ecosystem, OpCon operates using a frame-based system: every job, action, or data piece is handled as a distinct unit called a “frame.”

  • Creating Rules:

    • Operators write rules that define how Hownd should interact with data on websites. This involves:

      • Locating Data: Finding specific elements or data fields.

      • Grouping and Validating: Organising data meaningfully and ensuring its accuracy.

    • These rules eventually form the Blueprints that guide Hownd’s behavior:

      • Global Blueprints: Rules that apply universally to all websites or contexts.

      • Context Blueprints: Custom rules tailored for specific banks, services, or contexts.\

    • Operators go to great lengths to ensure that specific rules are added to explicitly handle and exclude user-specific data and other ‘dynamic’ data (such as advertisements). This is to prevent Hownd from misinterpreting or mishandling sensitive information and to keep the ‘frameDef count’ as low as possible.

  • Monitoring Jobs:

    • The Operator Console provides operators with an overview of job progress and performance, including:

      • The status of job attempts and their success

      • Errors, failures and issues (grouped into ‘Problems’)

      • Detailed logs to track each step of a job’s execution

Hownd has two main areas: the Job Workflow and the Operator Console.

Job Workflow

This area handles the work that needs to happen to complete tasks that Hownd has been assigned.

Here’s how it works:

  • A Consumer (e.g. Credit Sense) tells Hownd what jobs need to be done and provides details and goals that need to be achieved for the job to be successful.

  • HD-Job is the main part responsible for actually doing the work.
    It can be thought of as the “project manager” for all the work: it organises everything, makes decisions, starts the jobs, and gathers the results.

  • HD-Blueprint contains the data that HD-Job needs to understand and execute each task. This includes any necessary rules, procedures, or contextual information that helps define how a specific job should be handled.

  • In order to complete its tasks, HD-Job needs access to web browsers. HD-Asset helps manage these resources and track their status, making sure they are available.

  • HD-Browser is like the "worker" that does specific online tasks.
    It opens web pages, collects information, and can respond to instructions like clicking links or filling forms.

Operator Console

The Operator Console is designed for users who need to monitor and manage Hownd’s activities.

It serves as the control center, where users can view the status of various tasks, check results and set rules that guide job processing.

  • HD-OperatorConsole is the interface where operators log in to control ongoing tasks and access results. Most importantly, operators can access the Repair page, where they define the rules that Hownd follows for task processing, which are used by HD-Blueprint to guide HD-Job in task execution.

  • The OperatorConsole is connected to the Job Workflow via the HD-OperatorAPI which acts as a bridge, connecting the technical processes running in the background with the Operator Console.

Further reading

  • https://credit-sense.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/ABT/pages/11336515653/HDV3+Product+Design+-+Operator+Console?atlOrigin=eyJpIjoiYTUzZjU5OWY4ZTAzNGEwNTlmNGI2Y2MzMzk0NWQ0NWMiLCJwIjoiYyJ9

1. Objectives

  • Enable rapid identification of issues specific to Hownd or Yodlee integrations.

  • Support phased rollout and operational stability by providing actionable, provider-specific metrics and alerts.

  • Facilitate side-by-side comparison and drill-down for overlap banks.

2. Metrics to Track (Per Bank and Provider)

For each bank (orgId) and for both Hownd and Yodlee, track the following metrics:

  • Job success rate

  • Login success rate

  • MFA challenge and completion rates

  • Average and P95 tick/total job duration

  • PDF request completion rate

  • Error categories (error, issue, failure, success, partial_success)

3. Dashboard Design

  • Side-by-side comparison: For banks supported by both Hownd and Yodlee, display metrics in parallel panels for immediate comparison.

  • Filtering: Allow filtering by bank, provider, and time window.

  • Drill-down: Enable drill-down views for MFA and PDF flows, and error categories.

  • Trend visualisation: Use line/bar charts to show trends over time for each metric.

4. Alerting Strategy

  • Bank-scoped thresholds: Set alert thresholds per bank and provider.

  • Key alert conditions:

    • Sudden increase in error rate for a single bank/provider.

    • Spike in MFA failures or abandonment.

    • Increase in “fallback to Hownd” rate (where applicable).

  • Alert routing: Ensure alerts are routed to the relevant operational/support teams with clear indication of affected provider and bank.

5. Implementation Steps

Description

Assigned

Risks/Mitigations

1

Review current New Relic dashboards and identify gaps in provider/bank granularity.

Risk: Incomplete data sources. Mitigation: Audit data pipeline.

2

Define and implement new metrics and dimensions (provider, bank/orgId) in telemetry.

Risk: Schema changes. Mitigation: Staged rollout, test in lower environments.

3

Build/modify dashboards for side-by-side and drill-down views.

Risk: Dashboard complexity. Mitigation: Use templates, iterate with feedback.

4

Configure alert policies with bank/provider-specific thresholds.

Risk: Alert fatigue. Mitigation: Tune thresholds, review after initial rollout.

5

Validate dashboards and alerts during phased rollout (per bank group).

Risk: Missed issues. Mitigation: Manual validation, operator console checks.

6

Document dashboard usage and alert response procedures.

Risk: Knowledge gaps. Mitigation: Training sessions, update runbooks.

6. Ongoing Review

  • After each rollout phase, review dashboard effectiveness and alert accuracy.

  • Adjust metrics, thresholds, and visualisations based on production learnings and incident reviews.

  • Finalise documentation and ensure BAU processes reflect the new monitoring approach.

Overview

1. Goal and Job Context

The Goal (🥅) represents the overarching mission Hownd is trying to achieve. It is the consumer-defined end result tied to a Job request.

A Job (🚀) represents a request to fulfil a set of Objectives (the Goal) within a particular Context. When a consumer initiates a Job, they provide this Goal and Context. A single Job Attempt (🛫) is an execution aimed at fulfilling all the Objective Requests associated with that Job Instance.

2. Objective Definition and Instance

Objective Definition (The Blueprint)

The Objective (🔰 or ObjectiveDef) is a fundamental concept in Hownd, representing a single, high-level task or outcome that Hownd is designed to accomplish on behalf of a Consumer. It is essentially a piece of configuration defined in the Blueprint by Operators.

The Objective Definition must provide three key pieces of information, which can vary depending on the specific Context (the target system/website):

  1. Decisions/Plans: Information about which Decisions (or Plans—sequential groups of Instructions) Hownd must execute to further the Objective. For example, a Login Objective requires Plans covering "Entering a username and password" and "Clicking submit".

  2. Results: Hownd must be configured to recognise the possible outcomes of an Objective. A Result is a code representing the objective's final state when Hownd cannot proceed further, such as 'Success', 'Account Locked', or 'Unknown Error'.

  3. Dependencies: Constraints defining the execution order. If Objective A relies on Objective B (e.g., getting account data depends on successful authentication), Objective B must be completed first. This enforces an Invariant Execution Order constraint.

Objective Instance (The Runtime)

An Objective Definition becomes an Objective Instance during the runtime of a Job. An Objective Instance represents the total effort to fulfil that single Objective Request.

A key design aspect is the "For Each" principle: if a task must be repeated for multiple data items (e.g., "For each Account: Read the Account Information"), it requires a separate Objective Definition. These "For each" Objective Instances are generated dynamically during the Job execution once the necessary Model Instances (like the list of accounts) appear in the system state.

3. Objective Lifecycle States and Job Attempt Progression

Objectives drive the execution of a Job Attempt, primarily through the Think Phase (part of the continuous See/Think/Act cycle). The statuses of an Objective Instance track its progress through its internal lifecycle:

Objective State

Description

Relation to Job Flow

INITIALISED

The Objective has been created but has not yet passed through the primary processing function (objectiveThink).

Starting state.

PENDING

The Objective is constrained and should not take action, usually waiting for a prerequisite (Dependency) to complete (e.g., waiting for Authentication to succeed).

Prevents wasteful computation by checking Pertinence.

ACTIVE

The Objective is cleared to proceed and should execute Plans to fulfil its goal.

Leads to Decision making in the Act Phase.

RETIRED

The Objective has achieved a Result but continues to check subsequent States (Frames) to ensure its result remains valid.

Used for result validation against continuous website changes.

DONE

The final successful state. Achieved when the Objective is RETIRED and has successfully retrieved all the specific Values it was configured to track, with no incomplete descendants.

Indicates completion of a key milestone within the Goal.

The Role of Pertinence

In the Think phase, the process determines if an Objective is Pertinent (relevant) to the latest change in the system state (the incoming Frame/State).

  • If an Objective is configured to wait for another to complete (a Dependency), it will be marked as Passive (not pertinent) until that condition is met.

  • When deemed Pertinent, the Objective is passed to the Act Phase to request Decisions (Plans) to move the Job forward.

4. Relationship to Job Attempt Status

The final outcome of a Job Attempt is determined by aggregating the Results and Verdicts of all its individual Objectives.

Job Attempt Status Hierarchy

At the Job Attempt level, the status is categorized into three levels:

Level

Description

Examples

Verdict

The broadest outcome classification.

Success, Failure (external cause, e.g., incorrect credentials), or Error (internal application issue, goal is 0%).

Status

The specific stage in the lifecycle (used internally to drive logic).

ACTIVE, PENDING.

Result

The most specific outcome description, derived from Objective outcomes.

total_success or partial_success.

Objective Aggregation and Verdicts

The most critical link for a developer is understanding how the failure of an individual Objective affects the overall Job Attempt Verdict, which is managed by a configuration property called verdictAggregation:

  1. Critical: If an Objective marked as Critical fails, it causes the overall Job Attempt to result in an aggregated Failure.

  2. Insignificant: If an Objective marked as Insignificant fails (e.g., a non-essential piece of data is missing), the Job Attempt may still conclude as a Success but with the specific Result of partial_success.

  3. Ignored: The Objective's outcome has no bearing on the overall Job Verdict.

If all Objectives conclude without a Critical Failure, the Job Attempt can achieve total_success (all goals accomplished).

How to Run Tasks

  1. Press Ctrl+Shift+P (or Cmd+Shift+P on Mac)

  2. Type "Tasks: Run Task"

  3. Select the task you want to run from the list

Alternatively, use the keyboard shortcut Ctrl+Shift+B for build tasks.

Available Tasks

🚀 APP: Lifecycle (start, stop, clean, logs, config, endpoints)

Purpose: Manage the application lifecycle in the Kubernetes dev environment

Operations Available:

  • Start - Starts the application pod in the dev environment

  • Stop - Stops the running application pod

  • Clean - Cleans up application resources and temporary files

  • Logs - Displays real-time logs from the application pod

  • Config: Rebuild - Rebuilds the application configuration from HELM values

  • Endpoints - Shows available service endpoints and URLs

When to Use:

  • Starting your development session

  • Viewing application logs for debugging

  • Stopping services when done for the day

  • Troubleshooting configuration issues

Note: This task runs commands on the host machine via SSH to manage Kubernetes pods.

🔐 APP: Secrets

Purpose: Manage Kubernetes secrets for the application

Operations Available:

  • Add Secret - Add a new secret to the dev environment

  • List Secrets - Display all available secrets

  • View Secret - View the contents of a specific secret

  • Refresh Secrets - Reload secrets from the cluster

  • Delete Secret - Remove a secret from the dev environment

When to Use:

  • Setting up API keys or credentials for local development

  • Troubleshooting authentication issues

  • Managing environment-specific configuration

Security Note: Secrets are managed through Kubernetes and stored securely in the cluster.

🔌 POD: Port Forward to Pod

Purpose: Establishes port forwarding from your dev container to the application pod

Details:

  • Forwards port 9229 (Node.js debugging port) to your local environment

  • Automatically finds the first pod matching your project name

  • Kills any existing port-forward processes before establishing new connection

  • Runs in background mode

When to Use:

  • Attaching a debugger to the running application

  • Remote debugging Node.js processes in the pod

Troubleshooting:

  • If port forwarding fails, check that your pod is running

  • Verify you have the correct Kubernetes context selected

🔄 ENV: Update from HELM

Purpose: Synchronizes environment variables from HELM chart to your local .env file

What it Does:

  1. Backs up your current .env file

  2. Retrieves environment variables from the Kubernetes pod

  3. Updates the .env file with cluster configuration

  4. Cleans up temporary test reports

When to Use:

  • After updating HELM values

  • When starting work on a new feature that requires updated config

  • After switching Kubernetes contexts

  • Before running tests or migrations

Note: This task is automatically run as a dependency for DB migrations and integration tests.

📝 DEVENV: Open Config Files

Purpose: Quick access to main configuration files

Files Opened:

  • env.yaml.j2 - Environment variable templates

  • build/deploy/helm/values-k8sdevenv.yaml.j2 - HELM values for dev environment

  • Makefile - Build and deployment commands

When to Use:

  • Modifying environment-specific configuration

  • Updating HELM chart values

  • Checking or updating Makefile targets

🗄️ DB: Migration

Purpose: Runs database migrations in the dev environment

What it Does:

  1. Updates environment variables from HELM (dependency)

  2. Switches to the correct Node.js version via NVM

  3. Executes database migration scripts (npm run migrate)

  4. Backs up migration configuration for reference

  5. Restores original .env file

When to Use:

  • After pulling new migration files from the repository

  • When setting up a new database instance

  • After creating new migration scripts

Important:

  • Ensure your database connection is properly configured

  • Migrations are run in the context of HD-LOCAL-<username> environment

✅ TEST: Unit

Purpose: Runs the unit test suite

What it Does:

  • Switches to correct Node.js version via NVM

  • Executes npm run test in the source directory

  • Generates test reports

When to Use:

  • Before committing code changes

  • During TDD (Test-Driven Development)

  • Verifying bug fixes

Requirements:

  • Node.js dependencies must be installed (npm install)

🧪 TEST: Integration

Purpose: Runs integration tests against the dev environment

What it Does:

  1. Updates environment variables from HELM (dependency)

  2. Switches to correct Node.js version via NVM

  3. Executes npm run test:integration

  4. Backs up environment configuration used during tests

  5. Cleans up test reports and logs

When to Use:

  • Testing end-to-end workflows

  • Verifying API integrations

  • Before deploying to higher environments

Note: Requires active connection to Kubernetes cluster with running services.

👁️ CODE: Transpile TypeScript (watch)

Purpose: Continuously transpiles TypeScript to JavaScript in watch mode

What it Does:

  • Monitors TypeScript source files for changes

  • Automatically compiles on file save

  • Provides real-time compilation feedback

When to Use:

  • During active development

  • When you need immediate feedback on TypeScript errors

  • Testing changes without full rebuild

Run in Background: This task runs continuously; stop it with Ctrl+C or by terminating the task.

🔍 LINT: TypeScript

Purpose: Checks TypeScript code for style and quality issues

What it Does:

  • Runs ESLint/TSLint on TypeScript files

  • Reports code style violations and potential bugs

  • Enforces project coding standards

When to Use:

  • Before committing code

  • During code review preparation

  • Fixing code quality issues

Integration: Linting rules are defined in tslint.json or ESLint configuration.

🔍 LINT: TSQL

Purpose: Lints T-SQL (database) scripts for quality and style

What it Does:

  • Validates SQL syntax and style

  • Checks database migration scripts

  • Ensures SQL best practices

When to Use:

  • After writing new database migrations

  • Before committing SQL changes

  • Reviewing database scripts

Environment Variables

Tasks operate in the context of:

  • HD_ENVIRONMENT: Set to HD-LOCAL-<username>

  • DEVCONTAINER_PROJECT: Your project name

  • DEVCONTAINER_USER: Your username

This release contains major breaking changes, and will more than likely disrupt traffic.

1. Overview

After overlap banks (at least the medium‑volume cohort) are stable:

  • Order by risk: start with lowest‑volume and simplest MFA/PDF flows.

  • For each bank:

    • Use a similar Stage 0–3 pattern, but with no Hownd fallback:

      • Stage 0: internal/synthetic only.

      • Stage 1: very small real‑user cohort (e.g. specific partner or internal beta).

      • Stage 2–3: controlled ramp by volume.

  • Because there’s no fallback, be more conservative with:

    • Error thresholds

    • Alert sensitivity

    • Communication to customers/partners.

1.1 Proposed order (by cohort):

  1. Cohort 1 – Medium‑volume, AU/NZ mix:
    Goal: validate typical flows, MFA non-MFA without massive support impact.

  1. Cohort 2 – Long tail / lower volume:
    Goal: broaden coverage and catch edge cases in lower‑traffic banks.

  1. Cohort 3 – Very low volume banks:
    Goal: Work through the long list of banks supported by Yodlee that are required.

Within each cohort, you still treat each bank separately for activation and rollback, but you move through the cohort in a controlled order.

Objectives

  • Ensure all features from the rollout are stable, documented, and fully integrated into BAU (business-as-usual) processes.

  • Identify and close any gaps in documentation, systems, or operational processes.

  • Confirm that all success criteria for API support are met and that no critical outstanding work remains.

Key Activities

Description

Assigned

Risks / Mitigations

1

Review all rollout phases and confirm all features are live and stable for every bank/provider.

Risk: Missed edge cases. Mitigation: Use metrics, incident logs, and operator feedback.

2

Audit and finalise all technical documentation, including:

  • API reference

  • Integration guides

  • Error handling and troubleshooting

  • Runbooks and support procedures

Risk: Outdated docs. Mitigation: Cross-check with latest configs and code.

3

Update and validate all monitoring, dashboards, and alerting to reflect the final state (Hownd/Yodlee, all banks).

Risk: Gaps in monitoring. Mitigation: Manual validation, review alert coverage.

4

Review and update all operational processes:

  • Incident response

  • Support handover

  • BAU ownership

  • Training for support teams

Risk: Knowledge gaps. Mitigation: Training sessions, updated runbooks.

5

Capture and prioritise any follow-up stories for further optimisation, automation, or test coverage.

Risk: Untracked work. Mitigation: Use a consolidation checklist and backlog review.

6

Confirm all success criteria for the rollout are met:

  • No outstanding critical issues

  • All documentation/processes up to date

  • Monitoring and alerting effective

Risk: Premature closure. Mitigation: Stakeholder review and sign-off checklist.

Deliverables

  • Finalised and published documentation (API, integration, support).

  • Updated dashboards and alerting, with clear ownership.

  • Updated runbooks and BAU processes.

  • List of any remaining optimisation or improvement stories.

  • Stakeholder sign-off on completion.

Ongoing Review

  • Schedule a post-consolidation review (e.g., after 2 weeks) to ensure no new issues have emerged and all processes are working as intended.

  • Adjust documentation, monitoring, or processes as needed based on feedback and incident reviews.

The Act phase is where Hownd takes action to move the Job forward.

It creates and sends plans to external systems to work on objectives, but only if there’s still work to do.

If everything is complete, Act wraps up the Job and sends the Job results to the Consumer.

How Does Frame Act Work?

  1. Checking Before Acting
    Before doing anything, the Act phase runs some checks to decide if it should proceed or skip the work:

    • Special Frames:
      Some frames, like Asset or Plan receipts, need special handling.

    • Any Objectives Left?
      Act only runs if there are still unfinished objectives. If nothing’s left to do, the Job is marked as complete and the Job results are sent to the Consumer.

  2. Making a Plan
    If there is outstanding Objectives and Act decides to proceed, it creates a plan to tackle the remaining objectives.

  3. Executing a Plan

Problem

Currently to give the operator the flexibility to have different behaviours for different components we have to create variants of the same action. For example type, native_type, native_type_only, native_type_no_validation. This can blow out into a large number of different action variants all requiring their own individual global plan def.

Solution

Give the the operator the ability to specify configuration for an action for a given component def. 

Examples include:

  • Setting a delay on a click action

  • Turning validation on or off for a type action

  • Selecting a recovery method for a recover action i.e. go back or reload

Ideally we should have a single ‘type’ action, for which we could then select attributes, i.e. type natively, use validation, use mouse to focus, delay. Reducing the number of different actions required also reduces the number of plan definitions required in the global blueprint.

Considerations

  • Action attributes relate to an action, however, should be selectively applied per component def. If we set delay on a click, it should only be applied to the click action for that component Def.

  • Not all attributes will relate to all actions. For example, validation may be relevant for type action, but not for a recovery action.

  • An attribute may relate to many actions. I.e. delay may be set on any send action.

  • Hownd currently uses the action from the instruction rather than the componentDef, if an action is changed on an instruction in the global blueprint, howndbot will create a draft context blueprint and componentDefs may be updated to use the new action. Thus invalidating the action attributes previously selected for the component. Should these be deleted, ignored, applied to the new action..?

  • Sometimes we rely on different actions in order to create unique plans that will be executed, for example filter pre-condition in NAB. We currently rely on a native click filter pre-condition, followed by a click filter pre-condition. Before simplifying to a single click action we would need to address this scenario.

  • Context blueprint import / export / compare will need to include the new attributes.

  • Global blueprint import / export will need to include any action action attribute

  • Action attributes will need to be included when creating / deleting draft blueprints

Design

Choosing action attributes - Global blueprint

Operators will need to be able to identify which attributes are available for which actions. Actions attributes should be available in the action panel in the component builder on the repair page. Relevant attributes for a given action should be displayed, the appropriate control should be used for the attribute data type, i.e. checkbox, number input. sTables for action attribute information should be stored in the global blueprint.

<Rick insert mock up here>

Proposed global blueprint tables:

  • sActionAttributeType

  • sActionAttribute

Saving action attributes - Context Blueprint

Action attributes will need to be saved against a componentDef / action in the context blueprint. 

Proposed context blueprint table:

  • mComponentDefActionAttribute

Applying action attributes

Job

Action attributes need to be extracted from Blueprint, assigned to the appropriate instruction, and passed to Browser. Ideally these should not be persisted in Job.

Notes:

Extract from Blueprint -  ComponentDefGetManyExtract

Updated against a component - componentExtract

Stored in a map by componentDefId to be used when constructing instruction data

Added to the instruction to pass to browser - createWebPagePlan (prepareInstructionDataForPlan)?

Browser

Browser should receive action attributes on an instruction via browserPlanExecute. 

An amount of refactoring would be required to convert existing multi actions into single actions with configuration options. For new action attributes, only the work required to implement the new config is required.

Scope

Proposed initial scope is to introduce a single new attribute ‘delay’ and make it available for all send actions.

Level of support

Comments


Artefacts

Database Changes

  1. Created metric.mDataSource table (source/migrations/data/2025-10-28.metrics.dataSourceCode.sql):

CREATE TABLE metric.mDataSource (
    dataSourceCode VARCHAR(255)
)

  1. Added dataSourceCode column to metric.mSegment:

ALTER TABLE metric.mSegment
ADD dataSourceCode VARCHAR(255) NULL

  1. Created index for performance:

CREATE NONCLUSTERED INDEX IX_mSegment_dataSourceCode
ON metric.mSegment (dataSourceCode)
WHERE dataSourceCode IS NOT NULL

Stored Procedure Updates

Updated metric.pMetricUpdate to handle dataSource segments:

  1. Segment retrieval (lines 180-230):

-- Include dataSource segments in active segment list
SELECT s.segmentId, s.segmentType, s.segment, s.dataSourceCode, ...
FROM metric.mSegment s
WHERE s.isActive = 1
AND s.segmentType = 'dataSource'

  1. Segment management (lines 340-370):

-- Merge logic to add/update/deactivate dataSource segments
MERGE #mSegment dest
USING (
    SELECT DISTINCT
        'dataSource' segmentType,
        v.dataSourceCode segment,
        v.dataSourceCode dataSourceCode
    FROM metric.mDataSource v
) src ON src.segmentType = dest.segmentType 
    AND src.dataSourceCode = dest.dataSourceCode
-- ... merge logic

  1. Metric calculation (lines 600-650):

-- Calculate top 100 job attempts per dataSource
, cDataSource AS (
    SELECT rs.runSegmentId, x.jobAttemptId
    FROM #mRunSegment rs
    CROSS APPLY (
        SELECT TOP (100) ja.jobAttemptId
        FROM job.mJobAttempt ja
        WHERE ja.dataSourceCode = se.dataSourceCode
        ORDER BY ja.createDt DESC
    ) x
    WHERE se.segmentType = 'dataSource'
)

Data Population

The metric.mDataSource table is populated with distinct data source codes from job attempts, allowing the metric calculation to segment by data source (e.g., Yodlee, CBA, OpenBanking).

Bank known to use Akamai:

WBC - Westpac

BOQ - Bank of Queensland

BEN - Bendigo

UBCUR - UBank

Why is Akamai blocking me?

  • Akamai does not block users from accessing our customers' websites. However, our customers can use tools and policies which may in turn block you (the end user). Our customers use these rules to protect them and you from malicious actors on the internet. Some common reasons could include:Explicit IP blocking / blacklisting

  • Location-based backlisting

  • Rule-based blocking (i.e. web application firewall protections)

  • Reputation-base blocking

  • HTTP request rate controls (e.g. DoS protections)

  • The following activities may trigger application security controls:Web application layer attacks such as: SQL Injection, Cross-Site Scripting, Local File Inclusion, Remote Command Execution, Remote File Inclusion, etc.

  • Volumetric attacks or similar high rate HTTP traffic

  • Web contents scraping, data mining, web content indexing and similar automated web activities

  • Web vulnerability scanning using automated tools

Your reputation follows you. If your IP is identified as behaving poorly on one site, you may be blocked on other websites. A first step in troubleshooting may be to determine whether your IP Address is performing one of the activities listed above that could affect your reputation.Why is my IP Address assigned bad reputation?
Client Reputation leverages advanced algorithms to compute a risk score based on prior behavior as observed over the Akamai network. An IP address is assigned with bad reputation when Akamai observes malicious activity originating from this IP addressWhere can I find more information on Client Reputation?
Additional information on Client Reputation can be found at: https://www.akamai.com/solutions/security

 19 Dec 2024

The following banks were blocked:

  • WBC

  • BOQ

  • BEN

  • UBCUR

Fingerprint changes:

  1. Updated user agents, to include after seeing a page on WBC saying that only Windows and Macs were supported (see list of agents below)

    1. Update user agents - still blocked

  2. Bypass the Man In the Middle (MITM) proxy:

    1. Switch off - still blocked

    2. Switch on again - still blocked

  3. Fix Timezone bug for AU proxy

    1. Rolled out fix that set the timezone to Australia/Sydney when using the AU proxy - still blocked

  4. Change proxy region to NZ - unblocked

This could mean one of two things:

  1. AWS IP addresses are being blocked

  2. Out specific IP addresses are being blocked

Refreshing the IP Addresses on the Australian proxy seemed to resolve the issue.
