Potential issues:

  1. Very large data set returned

    1. We can change the output

  2. lots of objectiveIdentities to iterate over

  3. See Below…

In Node.js, every piece of work, whether it’s reading from the network, processing data, or running logic passes through a single event loop. HTTP requests don’t literally block the loop, but each one adds a promise that must later be handled when the response arrives.

With hd-job making many HTTP calls per frame, across many jobs running at once, the event loop quickly fills with pending responses. On top of that, many of these requests are coded to run in sequence (one must finish before the next starts). The result is that the event loop spends most of its time waiting for and resolving network responses, leaving less time for the actual data processing work. In effect, the system becomes I/O-bound rather than CPU-bound, and throughput drops as the backlog of requests grows.

It’s like having one cashier who can only serve one customer at a time — every blueprint request is another person in line, and when 25 people per job keep showing up, the line grows faster than the cashier can work through it.

The hd-job service makes heavy use of blueprint HTTP calls, with 40+ distinct endpoints across the codebase. These calls are a major performance bottleneck and significantly impact the Node.js event loop.

Call Frequency

  • Per Job Attempt: ~6–8 calls for initialisation/setup

  • Per Frame: ~8–12 calls across the See → Think → Act phases

  • Per Extraction: ~3–4 calls for definitions

In total, a typical job execution involves 15–25 blueprint calls, often taking 50–300 ms each, depending on data complexity. That means a single job attempt can spend 2–7 seconds waiting on blueprint responses.

How HTTP Works

A single HTTP request or response, especially with a large payload, will be broken down into many TCP packets. For a 10 MB payload, you'd send approximately 7,183 packets.

For a clearer visual representation of multiple data packets being exchanged during an HTTP transfer, I'll extend the data transfer phase.

 Client (Service A)                                      Server (Service B)

1. Client -> Server: TCP SYN packet (wants to connect)
2. Client <- Server: TCP SYN-ACK packet (acknowledges, ready for connection)
3. Client -> Server: TCP ACK packet (connection established)

                   [TCP Connection Established]

4. Client -> Server: HTTP GET /large-data (request for large data)
5. Client <- Server: TCP ACK packet (acknowledges receipt of request)

                   [Server preparing to send large data]

6. Client <- Server: TCP Data Packet #1 (part of HTTP response)
7. Client -> Server: TCP ACK packet (acknowledges Data Packet #1)
8. Client <- Server: TCP Data Packet #2 (part of HTTP response)
9. Client -> Server: TCP ACK packet (acknowledges Data Packet #2)
10. Client <- Server: TCP Data Packet #3 (part of HTTP response)
...
22. Client <- Server: TCP Data Packet #9 (part of HTTP response)
23. Client -> Server: TCP ACK packet (acknowledges Data Packet #9)
24. Client <- Server: TCP Data Packet #10 (final part of HTTP response)
25. Client -> Server: TCP ACK packet (acknowledges Data Packet #10)

                   [HTTP Response Fully Received]

26. Client -> Server: TCP FIN packet (wants to close connection)
27. Client <- Server: TCP ACK packet (acknowledges FIN)
28. Client <- Server: TCP FIN packet (server also wants to close)
29. Client -> Server: TCP ACK packet (acknowledges server's FIN)

                   [TCP Connection Closed]

As opposed to redis:

Redis handles data in a different way than HTTP. Rather than a request-response cycle per packet, Redis commands are much more lightweight. For a large payload like 10 MB, the process is streamlined. The Redis client and server establish a single connection, and then the client sends the large data in one command, which the server processes. The underlying TCP protocol will still break the 10 MB into many packets, but from the application (Redis) perspective, it's a single operation. The diagram below shows this high-level view.

 Client (Service A)                         Redis Server

1. Client -> Server: TCP SYN packet (wants to connect)
2. Client <- Server: TCP SYN-ACK packet (acknowledges, ready for connection)
3. Client -> Server: TCP ACK packet (connection established)

                   [TCP Connection Established]

4. Client -> Server: Redis SET key value (10 MB payload)
5. Client <- Server: TCP ACK packet (acknowledges receipt of payload)
6. Client <- Server: OK (Redis command executed successfully)

                   [Data Transfer Complete]

Blocking Behaviour

Many calls are sequential and blocking, compounding delays.
For example, in frameSeeProcess.ts, the system must fetch:

  • wrapperDefGetManyExtract

  • componentDefGetManyExtract

  • relevanceGroupDefGetManyExtract

…before extraction can even begin. This creates a cascading delay, as each frame cycle is gated by network I/O. The HTTPOptions('blueprint', 'post') configuration also includes retry logic, which can further multiply delays during service slowness or outages.

Event Loop Starvation and Concurrency Impact

Because these calls use await post(), the event loop is blocked while waiting for responses. In high-throughput scenarios, this leads to event loop starvation:

  • CPU-bound operations (like wrapper/component extraction) are delayed.

  • Adding child processes doesn’t help, since the bottleneck is blueprint I/O, not CPU load.

  • In fact, serialising large blueprint responses to child processes may increase overhead.

Key Insight

The current design is I/O-bound, not CPU-bound. The main bottleneck is waiting on blueprint data, not executing extraction logic.

Potential Improvements

  • Caching: Store and reuse frequently accessed blueprint data.

  • Pre-fetching: Load required definitions earlier in the pipeline.

  • Local Proxy Service: Reduce latency and retry overhead by colocating a blueprint cache/proxy with hd-job.

Shared Cache (Redis) Between Job and Blueprint

To reduce the performance bottleneck from repeated and sequential HTTP calls between hd-job and blueprint, a shared Redis cache can store and reuse frequently accessed blueprint data. Redis offers compact binary communication, making cache lookups significantly faster than HTTP requests.

How It Works

Blueprint-Managed Cache

  • Blueprint owns cache life-cycle: adding entries, defining expiry, and invalidating stale data.

  • Job does not write or modify cache for blueprint data — it only queries using known keys.

Expiry Control

  • Each cache entry has a blueprint-defined TTL (e.g. 90 minutes).

  • Redis automatically invalidates entries once expired, ensuring freshness without manual cleanup.

Data Retrieval

  • Job queries Redis for the most recent, valid data.

  • If data is expired or missing, the request falls back to a fresh blueprint HTTP request.

Cache Invalidation

  • Controlled entirely by blueprint, ensuring data accuracy and consistency.

  • Expired or invalidated entries are never returned to jobs.

Overview

Detect breaking changes in outgoing API responses (SEND instructions) between two Global Blueprint versions by comparing their database records, without generating or parsing API specs.

If there are breaking changes:

  1. The right hand draw is displayed with potential breaking changes

  2. Operator should run a job with the draft Global Blueprint ID

Concept

The CONSUMER domain’s ObjectiveDefs define all outgoing response structures.
Each SEND PlanDef under an ObjectiveDef specifies the shape of the JSON payload.
Each InstructionDef under that PlanDef maps to a field with an Identity that defines the property name and type.

So you can think of each Blueprint version as defining a tree of:

ObjectiveDef
 └─ PlanDef (SEND)
     └─ InstructionDef
         └─ Identity (code, type)

That’s all you need to compare between two versions.

Core Idea

Instead of building an OpenAPI spec, you can:

  1. Extract flattened SEND structure snapshots from two Global Blueprint versions:

    • Version A (previous published)

    • Version B (candidate about to be published)

  2. Compare their InstructionDef → Identity mappings:

    • Missing Identities = field removals (breaking)

    • Changed Identity type = type change (breaking)

    • Changed ObjectiveDef type = structure change (breaking)

    • Added Identities = safe (non-breaking)

  3. Report differences and block publication if breaking changes are found.

This would hook into the existing Global Blueprint publish process once the stats is changed to PUBLISHED.

Existing Diff System

There is an existing Global Blueprint compare system [dbo].[pCompareGlobalBlueprint]. Here is how a similar approach could be:

 Example stored procedure
CREATE OR ALTER PROCEDURE [dbo].[pDetectApiBreakingChanges]
    @fromGlobalBlueprintId UNIQUEIDENTIFIER,
    @toGlobalBlueprintId UNIQUEIDENTIFIER
AS
BEGIN
    SET NOCOUNT ON;
    SET XACT_ABORT ON;
    
    -- Reuse pCompareGlobalBlueprint logic
    -- But focus on CONSUMER domain SEND instructions

    -- Execute the comparison procedure to populate temp tables
    EXEC [dbo].[pCompareGlobalBlueprint] 
        @fromGlobalBlueprintId = @fromGlobalBlueprintId,
        @toGlobalBlueprintId = @toGlobalBlueprintId;
    -- #compare is created in pCompareGlobalBlueprint
    -- Now #compare, #compareChange, and #gb_* tables exist
    
    -- Create breaking change classification table
    DROP TABLE IF EXISTS #breakingChanges;
    CREATE TABLE #breakingChanges
    (
        breakingChangeId INT IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
        severity VARCHAR(20), -- BREAKING, DEPRECATION, NON_BREAKING, METADATA
        category VARCHAR(50), -- RESPONSE_FIELD_REMOVED, RESPONSE_TYPE_CHANGED, etc.
        entityType VARCHAR(50), -- ObjectiveDef, InstructionDef, Identity
        entityId VARCHAR(100),
        entityCode VARCHAR(255),
        description VARCHAR(1000),
        oldValue VARCHAR(1000),
        newValue VARCHAR(1000),
        recommendation VARCHAR(1000)
    );
    
    -- Analyse ObjectiveDef changes in CONSUMER domain
    INSERT #breakingChanges (severity, category, entityType, entityId, entityCode, description, oldValue, newValue, recommendation)
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN c.resultCode = 'deleted' THEN 'BREAKING'
            WHEN cc1.isArray != cc2.isArray THEN 'BREAKING'
            WHEN cc1.rangeFilterTypeCode != cc2.rangeFilterTypeCode THEN 'BREAKING'
            ELSE 'NON_BREAKING'
        END severity,
        CASE 
            WHEN c.resultCode = 'deleted' THEN 'RESPONSE_OBJECT_REMOVED'
            WHEN cc1.isArray != cc2.isArray THEN 'RESPONSE_STRUCTURE_CHANGED'
            WHEN cc1.rangeFilterTypeCode != cc2.rangeFilterTypeCode THEN 'RESPONSE_FILTER_CHANGED'
        END category,
        'ObjectiveDef' entityType,
        cc1.id entityId,
        cc1.objectiveDefCode entityCode,
        'ObjectiveDef ' + cc1.objectiveDefCode + ' was ' + c.resultCode description,
        CASE WHEN c.key1 IS NOT NULL THEN 'present' ELSE 'absent' END oldValue,
        CASE WHEN c.key2 IS NOT NULL THEN 'present' ELSE 'absent' END newValue,
        CASE 
            WHEN c.resultCode = 'deleted' THEN 'Maintain backwards compatibility by keeping this field in the response'
            WHEN cc1.isArray != cc2.isArray THEN 'Introduce API versioning to handle structure changes'
        END recommendation
    FROM #compare c
    JOIN #gb_objectiveDef cc1 ON c.key1 = cc1.[key] OR c.key2 = cc1.[key]
    LEFT JOIN #gb_objectiveDef cc2 ON c.key2 = cc2.[key] AND c.key1 = cc1.[key]
    WHERE c.compareObject = 'gb_objectiveDef'
        AND cc1.domainTypeCode = 'CONSUMER'
        AND c.resultCode IN ('deleted', 'changed');
    
    -- Analyse SEND InstructionDef changes
    INSERT #breakingChanges (severity, category, entityType, entityId, entityCode, description, oldValue, newValue, recommendation)
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN c.resultCode = 'deleted' AND cc1.importanceCode = 'REQUIRED' THEN 'BREAKING'
            WHEN c.resultCode = 'deleted' THEN 'DEPRECATION'
            WHEN ch.colName = 'importanceCode' AND ch.value2 = 'REQUIRED' AND ch.value1 != 'REQUIRED' THEN 'BREAKING'
            WHEN ch.colName = 'actionCode' THEN 'BREAKING'
            ELSE 'NON_BREAKING'
        END severity,
        CASE 
            WHEN c.resultCode = 'deleted' THEN 'RESPONSE_FIELD_REMOVED'
            WHEN ch.colName = 'importanceCode' THEN 'RESPONSE_FIELD_REQUIREMENT_CHANGED'
            WHEN ch.colName = 'actionCode' THEN 'RESPONSE_FIELD_ACTION_CHANGED'
        END category,
        'InstructionDef' entityType,
        cc1.id entityId,
        cc1.identityCode entityCode,
        'Field ' + cc1.identityCode + ' in ' + cc1.objectiveDefCode + ' was ' + 
        CASE WHEN c.resultCode = 'deleted' THEN 'removed' ELSE ch.colName + ' changed' END description,
        COALESCE(ch.value1, 'present') oldValue,
        COALESCE(ch.value2, 'absent') newValue,
        CASE 
            WHEN c.resultCode = 'deleted' AND cc1.importanceCode = 'REQUIRED' 
                THEN 'This is a BREAKING change. Add field back or version the API'
            WHEN c.resultCode = 'deleted' 
                THEN 'Mark as deprecated in v' + @currentVersion + ', remove in v' + @nextMajorVersion
            WHEN ch.colName = 'importanceCode' AND ch.value2 = 'REQUIRED'
                THEN 'Making a field required is BREAKING. Consider adding a default value'
        END recommendation
    FROM #compare c
    JOIN #gb_instructionDef cc1 ON c.key1 = cc1.[key] OR c.key2 = cc1.[key]
    LEFT JOIN #compareChange ch ON ch.compareId = c.compareId
    WHERE c.compareObject = 'gb_instructionDef'
        AND cc1.actionCode = 'SEND'  -- Only Analyse response fields
        AND (c.resultCode = 'deleted' OR ch.colName IN ('importanceCode', 'actionCode'));
    
    -- Analyse Identity type changes
    INSERT #breakingChanges (severity, category, entityType, entityId, entityCode, description, oldValue, newValue, recommendation)
    SELECT 
        'BREAKING' severity,
        'RESPONSE_FIELD_TYPE_CHANGED' category,
        'Identity' entityType,
        cc1.id entityId,
        cc1.identityCode entityCode,
        'Field type for ' + cc1.identityCode + ' changed from ' + ch.value1 + ' to ' + ch.value2 description,
        ch.value1 oldValue,
        ch.value2 newValue,
        'Type changes are BREAKING. Introduce new field with new type or create new API version' recommendation
    FROM #compare c
    JOIN #gb_identity cc1 ON c.key1 = cc1.[key] OR c.key2 = cc1.[key]
    JOIN #compareChange ch ON ch.compareId = c.compareId
    WHERE c.compareObject = 'gb_identity'
        AND ch.colName = 'identityType'
        AND ch.value1 != ch.value2;
    
    -- Return results
    SELECT 
        severity,
        category,
        entityType,
        entityCode,
        description,
        oldValue,
        newValue,
        recommendation,
        COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY severity) as countBySeverity
    FROM #breakingChanges
    ORDER BY 
        CASE severity 
            WHEN 'BREAKING' THEN 1 
            WHEN 'DEPRECATION' THEN 2 
            WHEN 'NON_BREAKING' THEN 3 
            ELSE 4 
        END,
        category,
        entityCode;
    
    -- Summary report
    SELECT 
        severity,
        COUNT(*) as changeCount
    FROM #breakingChanges
    GROUP BY severity
    ORDER BY 
        CASE severity 
            WHEN 'BREAKING' THEN 1 
            WHEN 'DEPRECATION' THEN 2 
            WHEN 'NON_BREAKING' THEN 3 
            ELSE 4 
        END;
END
GO
 Example Operation
import { Essentials, GraphQL, Prop, ClassValidator } from '@hownd/lib-hd-chassis'
import { Config } from '../../../Config'

const { IsString, IsEnum } = ClassValidator

export enum BreakingChangeSeverity {
    BREAKING = 'BREAKING',
    DEPRECATION = 'DEPRECATION',
    NON_BREAKING = 'NON_BREAKING',
    METADATA = 'METADATA'
}

export enum BreakingChangeCategory {
    RESPONSE_OBJECT_REMOVED = 'RESPONSE_OBJECT_REMOVED',
    RESPONSE_FIELD_REMOVED = 'RESPONSE_FIELD_REMOVED',
    RESPONSE_STRUCTURE_CHANGED = 'RESPONSE_STRUCTURE_CHANGED',
    RESPONSE_FIELD_TYPE_CHANGED = 'RESPONSE_FIELD_TYPE_CHANGED',
    RESPONSE_FIELD_REQUIREMENT_CHANGED = 'RESPONSE_FIELD_REQUIREMENT_CHANGED',
    REQUEST_FIELD_REQUIRED_ADDED = 'REQUEST_FIELD_REQUIRED_ADDED',
    REQUEST_FIELD_REMOVED = 'REQUEST_FIELD_REMOVED'
}

export class ApiSpecDetectBreakingChangesInput {
    /**
     * Original GlobalBlueprint ID
     * @example 1A57E61C-1EA8-4B82-99E0-1A89EBCC6A8D
     */
    @Prop.uuid()
    public fromGlobalBlueprintId!: string

    /**
     * New GlobalBlueprint ID to compare against
     * @example 2B68F72D-2FB9-5C93-A1F1-2B9AFBDD7B9E
     */
    @Prop.uuid()
    public toGlobalBlueprintId!: string
}

export class BreakingChange {
    @Prop(() => [{ type: String }])
    @IsEnum(() => [BreakingChangeSeverity])
    public severity!: BreakingChangeSeverity

    @Prop(() => [{ type: String }])
    @IsEnum(() => [BreakingChangeCategory])
    public category!: BreakingChangeCategory

    @Prop(() => [{ type: String }])
    @IsString()
    public entityType!: string

    @Prop(() => [{ type: String }])
    @IsString()
    public entityCode!: string

    @Prop(() => [{ type: String }])
    @IsString()
    public description!: string

    @Prop(() => [{ nullable: true, type: String, optional: true }])
    @IsString()
    public oldValue?: string | null

    @Prop(() => [{ nullable: true, type: String, optional: true }])
    @IsString()
    public newValue?: string | null

    @Prop(() => [{ type: String }])
    @IsString()
    public recommendation!: string

    @Prop(() => [{ type: String }])
    @IsString()
    public affectedEndpoint!: string
}

export class ApiSpecDetectBreakingChangesOutput {
    @Prop(() => [{ type: [BreakingChange] }])
    public breakingChanges!: BreakingChange[]

    @Prop(() => [{ type: Object }])
    public summary!: {
        breaking: number
        deprecation: number
        nonBreaking: number
        metadata: number
    }

    @Prop(() => [{ type: Boolean }])
    public hasBreakingChanges!: boolean

    @Prop(() => [{ type: String }])
    @IsString()
    public recommendation!: string
}

@GraphQL.Resolver()
export class ApiSpecDetectBreakingChangesOperation extends Essentials<Config> {
    /**
     * Detects breaking changes between two GlobalBlueprint versions
     * 
     * Analyses:
     * - Removed response fields (BREAKING if required)
     * - Changed field types (BREAKING)
     * - Changed response structures (BREAKING)
     * - New required request fields (BREAKING)
     * - Field deprecations (DEPRECATION)
     */
    @GraphQL.Query(() => [(returns) => ApiSpecDetectBreakingChangesOutput])
    public async apiSpecDetectBreakingChanges(
        @GraphQL.Args() input: ApiSpecDetectBreakingChangesInput
    ): Promise<ApiSpecDetectBreakingChangesOutput> {
        const breakingChanges: BreakingChange[] = []

        // Step 1: Get comparison data from database
        const comparisonResults = await this.mssql.exec<any>({
            data: {
                fromGlobalBlueprintId: { 
                    type: this.mssql.UniqueIdentifier(), 
                    value: input.fromGlobalBlueprintId 
                },
                toGlobalBlueprintId: { 
                    type: this.mssql.UniqueIdentifier(), 
                    value: input.toGlobalBlueprintId 
                }
            },
            isArray: false,
            isJSON: true,
            spName: 'global.pCompareGlobalBlueprint'
        })

        // Step 2: Analyse ObjectiveDef changes
        await this.analyseObjectiveDefChanges(comparisonResults, breakingChanges)

        // Step 3: Analyse InstructionDef changes (SEND = responses)
        await this.analyseSendInstructionChanges(comparisonResults, breakingChanges)

        // Step 4: Analyse InstructionDef changes (RECEIVE/READ = requests)
        await this.analyseReceiveInstructionChanges(comparisonResults, breakingChanges)

        // Step 5: Analyse Identity type changes
        await this.analyseIdentityChanges(comparisonResults, breakingChanges)

        // Step 6: Calculate summary
        const summary = {
            breaking: breakingChanges.filter(c => c.severity === BreakingChangeSeverity.BREAKING).length,
            deprecation: breakingChanges.filter(c => c.severity === BreakingChangeSeverity.DEPRECATION).length,
            nonBreaking: breakingChanges.filter(c => c.severity === BreakingChangeSeverity.NON_BREAKING).length,
            metadata: breakingChanges.filter(c => c.severity === BreakingChangeSeverity.METADATA).length
        }

        const hasBreakingChanges = summary.breaking > 0

        return {
            breakingChanges,
            summary,
            hasBreakingChanges
        }
    }

    private async analyseObjectiveDefChanges(comparisonResults: any, breakingChanges: BreakingChange[]) {
        // Analyse deleted or changed ObjectiveDefs in CONSUMER domain
        // Focus on SEND-related objectives (response structures)
    }

    private async analyseSendInstructionChanges(comparisonResults: any, breakingChanges: BreakingChange[]) {
        // Analyse changes to SEND instructions (response fields)
        // Breaking: Removed required fields, changed action codes
        // Deprecation: Removed optional fields
    }

    private async analyseReceiveInstructionChanges(comparisonResults: any, breakingChanges: BreakingChange[]) {
        // Analyse changes to RECEIVE/READ instructions (request fields)
        // Breaking: New required fields, removed fields
    }

    private async analyseIdentityChanges(comparisonResults: any, breakingChanges: BreakingChange[]) {
        // Analyse Identity type changes
        // Breaking: Type changes (string -> number)
    }
}

Modifying the Publish Process

When publishing the Global Blueprint we will display the right hand draw containing Global Blueprint issues listed. The Operator will need to scroll to the bottom of the list in order to continue the publish.

The draw is display as below, and will contain a list of potential breaking changes derived from the CONSUMER domain Global Blueprint.

If there are potential breaking changes, the Operator will then go to Main Menu, and run a job from there using the globalBlueprintId of the draft Global Blueprint. The output of this job can be checked to ensure that the results are expected.

Example Output

REMOVED: Account (Account Number) [string]

TYPE: Account (Balance) [number] → [string]

ADDED: Transaction (Pending) [boolean]

How are objectives completed?

Normal Plans

  1. FrameAct

    1. planExecuteMany

      1. executeAssetPlans

        1. TBC

      2. executeBrowserPlans

        1. If there plan succeeded then (i.e. browser returned no planStatusCode null), and there is NOT a plan.planTimeout then the plan is allocated the planStatusCode DONE

      3. executeConsumerPlans

        1. JSON payload is sent to the Consumer

          1. If there is an error sending the payload then the planStatusCode is set to FAILURE

          2. If the plan has NO plan.planTimeout then then plan receipt is included in the planReceipt which is a frame sent to frameStart.

        2. These plans are updated in FrameThink updatePlanByReceipts

    2. All other plans with plan.planTimeout are completed so that there is only one at anytime completeExistingSendPlansForDomain

Timeout plans

Plans with timeouts are allocated the PlanStatus WAITING_FOR_REPLACEMENT_PLAN.

  1. FrameAct:

    1. planExecuteMany

      1. executeAssetPlans

        1. TBC

      2. executeBrowserPlans

        1. If there plan succeeded then (i.e. browser returned no planStatusCode null), and there is a plan.planTimeout then the plan is allocated the planStatusCode WAITING_FOR_REPLACEMENT_PLAN(1)

      3. executeConsumerPlans

        1. JSON payload is sent to the Consumer

          1. If there is an error sending the payload then the planStatusCode is set to FAILURE

          2. If the plan has a plan.planTimeout then then plan receipt is not included in the planReceipts the assumption here is that the consumer will send a payload that will complete the plan.

          3. Otherwise a plan receipt is attached with the planStatusCode of DONE

        2. This means that the plan has to be completed either when:

          1. the consumer fulfills the data required by the plan, OR

          2. we send a plan receipt for the consumer payload the fuilfills the request

    2. All other plans with plan.planTimeout are completed so that there is only one at anytime completeExistingSendPlansForDomain

  2. FrameThink:

(1) Note that criticalPlan is the first plan found in planUpdates with the status WAITING_FOR_REPLACEMENT_PLAN.

Archive

To assist in determining why sites block Hownd v3, there are a number of configurations that can be adjusted per context. This includes setting up proxy configurations to mask the origin of requests and avoid IP-based blocking. Additionally, understanding and mitigating browser fingerprinting techniques, which track unique browser attributes to identify and block specific users, can be crucial. By fine-tuning these settings, you can better diagnose and address the reasons behind the blocks.

How to Update Fingerprinting Config

These configuration options can be adjusted from the contexts page. Note that you will need to create a Context Blueprint draft and publish config changes before new config will be used.

Note:

Preferred Proxy

The first change should be to adjust the proxy used.

  • Australia - DEFAULT / AU

  • New Zealand - NZ

Example config:

{
  "preferredProxy": "NZ"
}

Finger Print Config

Useful resources:

  • https://amiunique.org/fingerprint

This site exists in Hownd Stage and Production:

  • https://stg-hd-operatorconsole.creditsense.io/contexts?contextCode=AMIUNIQUE

User Agents

User agents can be updated and added to outside of the upgrade of the browser Howd uses to capture data. There are lists of popular user agents on the web, for example:

  • https://www.useragents.me

  • https://www.whatismybrowser.com/guides/the-latest-user-agent/chrome

It appears that success drops off for some sites such as PCFCC if the user agents become too out of date, therefore updating these regularly will typically improve the success rate.

Example configuration:

{
  "fingerPrintConfig": {
    "userAgents": [
      "Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/122.0.0.0 Safari/537.36",
      "Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/122.0.6261.156 Safari/537.36",
      "Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/124.0.0.0 Safari/537.36",
      "Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/125.0.0.0 Safari/537.36",
      "Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/126.0.0.0 Safari/537.36",
      "Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/127.0.0.0 Safari/537.36",

Example config:

{
  "fingerPrintConfig": {
    "browserPermissions": {
      "notifications": true,
      "fullscreen":true,
      "geolocation":true,
      "seconds":true,
      "geolocation": false
    }
  }
}

Proxy Options

There are a number of browser fingerprint items that are detectable when using the MITM proxy (this specifically involves removing a TLS Cipher and adding a different Cipher). The MITM proxy is responsible for capturing assets such as CSS, and images to display in the browser.

The options are:

  • off - talks directly to the website without any proxy involved

  • mitm (default) - use the MITM proxy, this means that requests go from Chrome through NodeJS and then an outgoing proxy such as the Australian ones

  • bypass-mitm - Keep the outgoing proxy but don’t use the MITM proxy

Example config:

{
  "fingerPrintConfig": {
    "proxyOptions": "bypass-mitm"
  }
}

Draft 2


We want an easy score, similar to Apdex, that tells us how our overall consumer experience is going.

Overview

ANZ NZ have implemented additional security to block automated access to their site in response to attacks they've been having. Because Hownd and Yodlee use automated access as part of our service, we've been caught up in this. This page contains information related to how they've implemented this so that we can overcome this and continue to provide our services.

Currently, Hownd v3 is getting an Access denied screen when we attempt to logon.

https://hd-operatorconsole.creditsense.io/repair?contextId=5F3600AD-F9E0-D0FA-511F-017D084E8B94&frameDefId=9E1045D0-569B-F33E-75C1-017D0C4E46E1&frameId=4DBC0A2D-1793-5A3B-A409-017D0C591EBB&jobAttemptId=6CAC79B9-1207-39C9-9AAC-017D0C58FD86

Schedule

Thursday and Friday (11th and 12th Nov) will include planning and information gathering and dev environment setup as prep.

Monday 15th will be a day dedicated to investigating and implementing mechanisms with the goal of understanding the mechanisms ANZ NZ is using to block access and to overcome these so that we can provide our service and insights to Yodlee to help them.

Goals

Key goals in order of priority:

  1. Run a HDv3 job and not be blocked
  2. Understand the changes that resulted in being successful
  3. Understand the changes that cause us to be blocked

Imperva

It is known that ANZ NZ have implemented "Imperva" software as part/all?? of their security mechanisms to block access.

https://www.imperva.com/


It is also suspected that the specific product being used is - Cloud Application Security (previously Incapsula).


Imperva - Documentation

Following contains information from Imperva's docs to gain a better understanding of the mechanisms that their software has in place to allow their clients (in this case ANZ NZ) write rules to restrict access to their website.

#DescriptionLinkKey insights
1Three Strike Rulehttps://docs.imperva.com/bundle/cloud-application-security/page/security-events.htm#Thethreestrikerule

"Imperva intelligently analyzes traffic in order to detect suspicious behavior. When a client sends repetitive, clearly malicious requests, Imperva may block this session to prevent zero-day attacks. The 3-strike rule occurs automatically when 3 requests in the same session trigger specific WAF rules that we have classified as extremely high-risk."

As we experiment, be aware we may trigger a larger block due to previous activity. This could easily lead to seemingly inconsistent response were sometimes it worked and then later it doesn't.

2Uses a rules based mechanismhttps://docs.imperva.com/bundle/cloud-application-security/page/rules/create-rule.htm

Based on creating rules, defining the criteria to trigger it and setting an action when triggered.

Actions can include:

  • Alert
  • Block Request
  • Block Session
  • Block IP - looks like this blocks for 10min
  • Require Cookie Support
  • Require Javascript Support
  • Require CAPTCHA Support
3Also have rate ruleshttps://docs.imperva.com/bundle/cloud-application-security/page/rules/rates.htmNot relevant for our initial problem (i.e., we get blocked straight up, not after a time)... after solving the first use case, this may also be something that we encounter
4Security Rule Use Case Exampleshttps://docs.imperva.com/bundle/cloud-application-security/page/rules/security-rule-examples.htm

Gives a good understanding of how they write specific rules to cover particular problems.


5List of Parameters to Filterhttps://docs.imperva.com/bundle/cloud-application-security/page/rules/rule-parameters.htm

Tells us what things they can leverage to define security rules.

Key parameters that are likely most relevant for us:

  • Client ID
  • Client Type

For region based restrictions, likely using:

  • Continent Code
  • Country Code
  • Client IP (they may use this to block particular ranges that relate to known services such as AWS)


Here's some others that are less relevant but may still matter:

  • Is Mobile
  • User Agent
  • Declared Client ID
  • Declared Client Type


6Exact error page we're seeing on Howndhttps://docs.imperva.com/bundle/cloud-application-security/page/error-codes.htm


7Bot Mitigation Overviewhttps://docs.imperva.com/bundle/cloud-application-security/page/settings/auto-mitigation.htmA good overview of how Imperva works in relation to us

Module used to wrap any request to a webpage blocked by incapsula.

  • This is old and comments suggest it no longer works, however it contains Incapsula insights
https://github.com/ziplokk1/incapsula-cracker-py3

Notes

  • As of now, this is only proven to work with the following sites:
    • whoscored.com
    • coursehero.com
    • offerup.com
    • dollargeneral.com
  • I understand that there's minimal commenting and that's because I'm not sure exactly why incapsula is sending requests to certain pages other than to obtain cookies. This is just a literal reverse engineer of incapsulas javascript code.
  • Feel free to contribute. Unfortunately webscraping is such a dynamic field that I can't always put out updates and make changes for specific sites. So I turn to the community to help with those issues. Thank you for your understanding. For anyone who is using this library and it works for your site, please send me a note so i can add it to the list.

How it works

Lets start with how incapsula works first:

  1. When you navigate to a webpage, incapsula runs some javascript code which tests your browser to see if it's using selenium, phantomJS, mechanize, etc.
  2. A cookie is created which holds the results of this test.
  3. A request is then sent out which "applies" the cookie and now sends back a few other cookies necessary to obtain access to the site.
  4. Any subsequent request is now authorized to access the site until the cookie expires.
  5. If there are too many requests being made to the site despite the cookie authentication, incapsula will serve back a re-captcha instead.

When detecting whether a resource is blocked, by default, we look for two elements.

<!-- element 1 -->
<meta name="ROBOTS"/>

<!-- element 2 -->
<iframe src="link to some incapsula resource"></iframe>

Finding both of these elements is necessary because unless both tags are present (from what I have seen) then the resource is not blocked.

Once we have determined that the resource is blocked, we send a get request to the src of the iframe to determine its contents.

If the contents contain a re-captcha, then there's nothing we can do and we raise a IncapBlocked exception. Otherwise we set the ___utvmc cookie, send the GET request to apply the cookie, and send a new request to the original url.

2Article talks about the above solution and how it came about and why it worked and what's happened since then.https://waf-bypass.com/how-hackers-community-cracked-imperva-anti-bot-solution/

As for now, incapsula-cracker is not up-to-date and can not cover up all the solution’s changes, but the idea behind this and the way of bypassing is really interesting and still the same – and that’s what we are looking forward to. 

Simply put, incapsula-cracker tries to re-implement part of the javascript logic delivered by Incapsula anti-bot solution on the client-side, but with Python, instead of browser appliance. For example, incapsula-cracker spoofs all the browser checkings by re-implementing JavaScript function on the python-cracker side – basically, incapsula-cracker sends all the browser flags (such as “navigator.webdriver”, which is works like a bot-flag, or special browser-related flags like “chrome”, “opera”, “yandex” and so on) manually, so it becomes impossible to Incapsula to detect if it sent from a browser or by another method. So, it’s easy for a cracker to bypass the browser check, even when it looks really complicated

Also, besides this, the cracker collects all the required parameters and JavaScript variables from the provided scripts just to calculate and process it the same way as it was with the browser flags. Basically, the cracker is able to use some regular expression for it, and then it can calculate it on the Python side too. So, here we have full logic re-implementation which can be handled on the side of the client, without any browser or JavaScript engine.

Our operators want to scope out new banks in the staging environment then transfer the blueprints to production. This process will involve exporting the blueprint to file then importing the blueprint into the next environment. This process will also lend itself to transfering blueprints from production to any developers environment for analysis, testing, etc.

Assumptions

Only published blueprints can be exported.

Blueprints will be imported as drafts.

An export contains all context blueprint data and sufficient context common data to facilitate the import.

Common Data

Which common data will be required?

  • mPage

  • mNavigate

  • mNavigateLink

  • mPageTag

  • mPagePageTag

The pages will be regenerated from the componentDef pageTags during import and all appropriate pagePageTag entries created.

Sufficient data will be exported to create any missing pageGroups (global common data) that are referenced by pageTags.

NavigateLinks are operator created links between pages and are transfered through the common context import process.

Hashes

Definition: A hash is calculated for a collection of componentDefs found on a frameDef.

The hash will be given a new boolean attribute of isTrusted.

When a component is created, updated, or deleted then all it’s associated hashes will become untrusted.

When a hash is seen during the See phase it will become trusted.

Hashes will no longer be deleted during the publish process. Instead, only trusted hashes will be copied into a new draft. This will ensure that the number of hashes is held in check.

Only trusted hashes will be exported and imported.

Navigation

Definition: ComponentDefs are assigned pageTags which are used to calculate a pageHash, aka a Page. Navigation is both the algorithm and the table required to navigate between those pages.

The current navigation algorithm requires knowledge of both hashes and frameDefs. This change will remove the requirement to know about frameDefs by relying on the trustworthiness of hashes.

Frames

During import, if the target environment already has a version of the context then all the frameDefs will be copied from the existing version to the new version. These frameDefs will be associated with the “null” hash (a hash of an empty set of componentDefs) during import. They will be associated with the correct hash during the See process.

ContextBlueprintPublish

REST POST: /contextBlueprintPublish

Purpose:

  1. Receive rest request to initiate publish process
  2. Create records in the database that represent each Exemplar Frame that needs to be evaluated
  3. Add a queue item for each Exemplar Frame to be evaluated (if there are 200 Exemplar Frames that need evaluating, then there will be 200 queue items)
  4. Set ContextBlueprint status to PUBLISHING

Special considerations:

  • Wait for publishing is required when running end-to-end tests, in that we must know when the context is published before starting any tests.

Clean up

Clean up of Exemplar frames occurs:

  • When frames are merged the newest frame becomes Obsolete

How:

  • Job makes a request to blueprint for X exemplar frames that need to be cleaned up
  • Blueprint queries the database for:
    • FrameDefs marked as obsolete
    • FrameDefs that do not exist in the currently published context blueprint
  • Job removes the frames for EFS
  • Job sends a success message back to blueprint
  • Blueprint removes the frameDefs from the database

ContextBlueprintPublishExemplarFrameConsumer

LOCAL: contextBlueprintPublishExemplarFrameConsumer

Purpose:

  1. Retrieve 1 ExamplarFramePublish item from the queue
  2. Run this frame through FrameDefEvaluate
  3. Store the results of FrameDefEvaluate in the database
  4. When all queue items are processed:
    1. Determine if there have been any issues
    2. If Passed:
      1. Update frameDef hashes 
      2. Publish the ContextBlueprint
    3. If Failed
      1. Update ContextBlueprint status to DRAFT again
    4. Inform the team success/fail

  2. Send a job through from Menu Menu with invalid credentials

Test 3: MITM TLS Signature

TLS Fingerprints

Objectives

  1. Determine if the fundamental TLS fingerprint is the reason for blocking.

2.1 Bypass MITM

The purpose of this test is to check if the specific TLS signature of NodeJS is what is being used to identify Hownd Browser.

{

  "fingerPrintConfig": {

  }
}

If Successful we should see jobs start to succeed again.

Test 4: HTTP Referrer

Objectives

Determine if HTTP Referrer is being used to fingerprint traffic and leading to being blocked.

4.1 Add HTTP referrer

export const generalReferrerUrls = [
	'https://facebook.com',
	'https://x.com',
	'https://instagram.com',
	'https://amazon.com',
	'https://live.com',
]
export function getReferrerUrl(url: string): string {
	// Define a mapping of country codes to potential referrer URLs
	const referrerUrls: { [key: string]: string[] } = {
		ca: ['https://www.google.ca', 'https://www.yahoo.ca', 'https://www.bing.com'],
		us: ['https://www.google.com', 'https://www.yahoo.com', 'https://www.bing.com'],
		uk: ['https://www.google.co.uk', 'https://www.yahoo.co.uk', 'https://www.bing.com'],
		// Add more country codes and corresponding URLs as needed
	}

	// Extract the country code from the incoming URL
	const countryCode = url.match(/\.(\w{2,3})(\/|$)/)?.[1]

	// Check if the country code has a corresponding list of referrer URLs
	if (countryCode && referrerUrls[countryCode]) {
		const urls = referrerUrls[countryCode].concat(generalReferrerUrls)
		// Select a random URL from the list for the given country code
		const randomIndex = Math.floor(Math.random() * urls.length)
		return urls[randomIndex]
	}

	const urls = referrerUrls.us.concat(generalReferrerUrls)
	// Select a random URL from the list for the given country code
	const randomIndex = Math.floor(Math.random() * urls.length)
	return urls[randomIndex]
}

Outcome

No discernible difference

Manually setting permissions will make Hownd Browser look slightly different than previous attempts. If this does succeed it’s likely that these attributes will be fingerprinted again, however it does provide information about which aspects of the browser are being used.

{

   "browserPermissions":{

      "geolocation":true,

   }
}

DNS Leak

What are WebRTC leaks?

Browser is configured to not expose WebRTC leaks

WebRTC implement STUN (Session Traversal Utilities for Nat), a protocol that allows to discover the public IP address. To disable it:

  • Mozilla Firefox: Type "about:config” in the address bar. Scroll down to “media.peerconnection.enabled”, double click to set it to false.

  • Google Chrome: Install Google official extension WebRTC Network Limiter.

  • Opera: Type "about:config" in the address bar or go to "Settings". Select "Show advanced settings" and click on "Privacy & security". At "WebRTC" mark select "Disable non-proxied UDP".

What are DNS leaks?

In this context, with "DNS leak" we mean an unencrypted DNS query sent by your system OUTSIDE the established VPN tunnel.

Why does my system leak DNS queries?

In brief: Windows lacks the concept of global DNS. Each network interface can have its own DNS. Under various circumstances, the system process svchost.exe will send out DNS queries without respecting the routing table and the default gateway of the VPN tunnel, causing the leak.

There were issues with MAQ Lee Sinclair collect the docs and put them here.

MAQ seems to have volume/time based blocking. It’s important to keep the Browser user agents up to date with MAQ.

Work arounds:

  1. The workaround code implementing a hard coded delay for NAB click action is removed from Browser (Rick Meredith Dain Drew please provide details source: [EPIC] - HND-6368 - (LS) - Hownd Scalable )

Example logs:

  • https://onenr.io/0VjYLrbl0R0

Working Sheet (Filtering to NAB)

Research on Fingerprinting

  • The technique for identifying returning browsers might be used to also detect bots. In that the web browsers used by Hownd are all on the same OS in the same timezone with the same fonts etc. Here is an example of this technique for fingerprinting:

    • https://fingerprint.com/demo/

Possible fingerprinting using audio

  • https://fingerprint.com/blog/bypassing-safari-17-audio-fingerprinting-protection/?utm_source=tldrnewsletter

Experiment #1 Reduce duplication of IP Address usage

The fuzzy pattern of IP Address re-use may indicate WAP firewall rules that are adaptive and designed to stop scraping.

The concept for testing this hypothesis is to add logic that will avoid re-using IP addresses if they have been used within the path 30 minutes.

HND-6044 - Getting issue details... STATUS

Test 1: Avoid re-using the same IP addresses / User Agents

Objectives

  1. Determine if the blocking is limited to IP addresses only.

Approach

This is a change to Browser, where the proxy IP addresses are recorded by asset and for each Context sent to browser so that when it chooses a proxy IP address it can avoid using one that has been used recently.

In this test we also included a random selection of User Agents from a limited list of Chrome versions near the electron

Failed

Test 2: Proxy Region

Objectives

  1. Determine if AWS-based IP addresses are being blocked.

2.1 Manually while being blocked

When blocking starts, switch to NZ proxy for NAB. and observe results.

While this approach to simple to implement, it does mean that jobs will error.

Testing will be conducted with 1.136.0 which has the following fixes:

  • Consistent User Agent

2.3 Approach #2: Randomisation (Requires significant change)

Since switching to a different proxy region didn’t effect the success rate, randomising between AU and NZ is unlikely to provide any further information.

 Details

In this test, we will randomly assign a proxy to a job within the AU/NZ regions for NAB.

This task will involve making changes to the following:

  • Pantry

  • Blueprint

  • Operator Console

  • Job

  • Browser

However, due to a bottleneck in releasing to production, it is likely that such a significant change will be stuck in staging.

Test 4: MFA Cookie

Objectives

  1. Determine if the MFA cookie can be used as a way to bypass MFA for all NAB accounts

  2. Determine if the MFA cookie can be used to bypass MFA for subsequent NAB logins

Approach

  1. Capture the cookie which can be read in the Firefox browser,

  2. Inject it into a local running hd-browser instance

  3. Test 4.1: Login to https://ib.nab.com.au/login with the account used to generate to Cookie

  4. Test 4.2: Login to https://ib.nab.com.au/login with a different account

  5. Test 4.3: Login to https://ib.nab.com.au/login with the account used to generate to Cookie

Outcomes

Test 4.1: Success

Test 4.2: Failure

Test 4.3: Success
